_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
TCEQ administers Title 30 Texas Administrative Code (30 TAC), which constitutes Texas environmental law across air quality, water quality, and waste management domains. The workspace, operating as a freight forwarder with Texas-based operations (air cargo gateways at DFW/IAH/SAT, road transport corridors, and cargo warehousing), sits in the compliance chain primarily as a **regulated facility operator and waste generator/transporter**, rather than as a producer or extended-producer-responsibility obligor.

**Air quality:** Any fixed facility operated by the workspace—warehouse HVAC systems with combustion equipment, emergency diesel generators, forklifts, refrigeration units using controlled substances, or truck loading docks in ozone nonattainment areas—may require an air permit by rule (30 TAC Chapter 106) or new-source review permit (30 TAC Chapter 116) depending on emission thresholds. The workspace is not a "major source" in most freight configurations, but stationary combustion or VOC-emitting operations in DFW, HGB, or Bexar County must be mapped against Chapter 115 (VOC) and Chapter 117 (NOX) controls. Air permit applicants are required to publish notice and provide plain-language summaries in accordance with 30 TAC Chapter 39 Subchapter H. *Role confirmation required:* Legal counsel should confirm whether any workspace facility triggers a New Source Review obligation.

**Waste:** Under 30 TAC Chapter 335, the workspace acts as a **waste generator** when handling packaging debris, damaged cargo, hazardous materials (e.g., lithium batteries, art chemicals, film set waste), and residual hydraulic fluids from automotive shipments. Generators must classify wastes at the point of generation and inform transporters of the waste classification; transporters must comply with applicable requirements for that waste class. If the workspace also arranges waste transport between Texas facilities, it assumes a **transporter** role under 30 TAC §§335.6(d), 335.11, and 335.91–335.94. TCEQ regulates hazardous and industrial waste transportation on public roads and rights-of-way in Texas.

**Vehicle fleet:** For workspace-operated ground transport in I/M program counties (DFW, HGB, ARR, El Paso, Bexar), the workspace is a **fleet registrant** subject to OBD emissions-inspection requirements under 30 TAC Chapter 114.

*Source: Current Rules and Regulations, TCEQ, February 2026. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/rules/current.html; Transporting Waste in Texas (RG-086), TCEQ. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/downloads/permitting/waste-registration/pub/rg-086.pdf*
